title: Let Us Continue Our Work Without Losing Heart at Defeat!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source page explicitly prints `2000 ரூபாய்` for each worker at Minnampalli in this letter; that figure is preserved even though an earlier letter reports a different amount. The isolated OCR fragment `Carga` is source-verified as `தேர்தல்`. Other confirmed repairs include `பேர்களைச்`, `இடைத்தேர்தலில்`, `ஆளுங்கட்சியின்`, `அதிகார துஷ்பிரயோகங்கள்`, `பாப்பநாயக்கன்பட்டி`, `மற்றும்`, `வீடுகளில்`, and broken word spacing. The printed salutation is restored. Canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: The Yercaud Assembly by-election has concluded. Even before nominations were filed, I issued a statement on 27 October 2013 about the lawlessness the ruling AIADMK was preparing to unleash. Have you ever heard of any Assembly by-election for which fifty-two election-committee organisers were first appointed and then nine more added, making sixty-one in all? Thirty-one of them were ministers in the cabinet. Together with the party presidium chairman, organisation secretaries, board chairpersons and wing secretaries, sixty-one people were appointed as election organisers.

In my “Udanpirappu” letter at the time, I wrote that it was doubtful whether we could withstand and overcome the lawlessness all these people would jointly unleash. That doubt has now proved true. That is all.
As soon as the party decided to field a DMK candidate in the Yercaud by-election, I personally wrote, in the proper manner, to the leaders of most parties in Tamil Nadu seeking their support. I said:

> We have decided to contest the Yercaud (ST) Assembly by-election to be held on 4 December 2013 on behalf of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam. We have taken this decision believing it constructive for all opposition parties to unite and extend firm support in this election, thereby warning the ruling AIADMK against the anti-democratic actions it continues to pursue in Tamil Nadu. To help the party's decision to defend democracy and defeat authoritarian thinking in Tamil Nadu, I request your party's support for the DMK candidate in this by-election.

The Congress and BJP replied that they would consult their national leadership and convey a decision. They never declared their support. Several others did not reply at all. But there can be no doubt that our party's frontline members, district secretaries, Udanpirappugal and comrades from allied parties worked tirelessly day and night. The truth, however, is that we could do nothing against the ruling party's lawlessness, the money it showered for votes and its ministers' abuse of authority.
Meanwhile, newspapers continuously reported everything done in the constituency by the ruling AIADMK with the District Collector's cooperation.

The Election Commission announced that the election code applied only to the constituency. Yet officials, including the Salem District Collector, said it applied throughout the district. On that basis, officials removed DMK flagpoles across Salem district without notice and without even informing the district party organisation. They took no action to remove AIADMK flagpoles.

Although the Collector announced that the election code had taken effect throughout Salem district from 5 October, the day the election was announced, free bicycles, laptops and other goods were distributed to schoolchildren in many villages within Yercaud, including Thumbal, Pappanaickenpatti and Vellalapatti. Were they distributed without the Collector's permission?

A complaint was lodged against party candidate V. Maran at the Vazhapadi police station for allegedly violating election rules. The Superintendent of Police said it had been lodged by the Vazhapadi Village Administrative Officer. The complaint alleged that more than ten vehicles accompanied the DMK candidate when he travelled from Vazhapadi to Attur to campaign on 22 October 2013. But the Superintendent said the officer had not explained why the complaint was filed three days later and had submitted no evidence that ten vehicles accompanied the candidate, such as a video showing their registration numbers.

Even after the code came into force, the AIADMK government carried out various welfare activities throughout Salem district, where Yercaud is located. District party in-charge Sivalingam complained to the Collector and senior district police officers, but the complaint was ignored and no action was taken.

The Election Commission ordered ministers visiting Salem district not to use government vehicles. Yet on 14 October 2013, Tamil Nadu's Health Minister travelled ostentatiously in a car bearing the state-government emblem and a red beacon. The party submitted a photographic complaint to the Election Commissioner.

Under the guise of inaugurating new buses in the state, attempts were made to use public money and the administrative machinery to imprint the AIADMK election symbol on those buses and influence the public.
On 30 October 2013, *Dinamalar* itself reported that a display compiling the AIADMK government's achievements had been placed for voters to see within the Salem Collectorate complex.

Jayalalithaa's image was not concealed on houses built under the Green Houses Scheme in Yercaud. Her picture appeared on mineral-water bottles, and government advertising in her favour was freely allowed everywhere. Boards advertising the AIADMK government's two-year achievements were displayed throughout the district, including at the Collector's office.

The party complained that instead of acting impartially as an election officer, the Collector openly behaved like an AIADMK district secretary. Like a fence devouring the crop it should protect, the very officer responsible for rigorously enforcing the election code conducted religious ceremonies and laid the foundation stone at 10.45 a.m. on 6 November 2013 for a 2.52-crore-rupee building for the Omalur Taluk office, opposite the old Omalur courthouse. The Salem District Revenue Officer, Mettur Sub-Collector, Omalur Tahsildar, Block Development Officer and Public Works Department assistant executive engineers took part. If the district's chief officer, who should safeguard the election code, treats it with contempt and violates it, to whom can one complain?

Only newly eligible eighteen-year-olds could be added to the supplementary electoral roll. Yet fifteen thousand bogus voters were announced as having been added, including forty-year-olds with no connection to the constituency who were falsely shown as residing at AIADMK members' addresses.
The DMK wrote to India's Chief Election Commissioner demanding their immediate removal.
Despite the Election Commission's order against using government vehicles or staying in government guest houses during campaigning, some ministers and board chairpersons violated it.
Because ministers served on the election committee, they made officials working under their departments act in support of the ruling-party candidate.
On 11 November, *Dinamalar* reported that government assistance goods such as mixers, grinders and fans had been brought into Yercaud for distribution to voters.
Officials confiscated the public's money in the name of vehicle checks on Yercaud's national highways, yet conducted no checks on village roads used by ministers.
When Tamil Nadu's Highways Minister arrived to file the nomination in an AIADMK flag-bearing car, police assigned to protect him violated election rules by using a Tamil Nadu government jeep.

When the AIADMK candidate came to file the nomination, more than forty cars flying AIADMK flags arrived in violation of the rules. At noon on 13 November, every minister and AIADMK Member of Parliament and Legislative Assembly arrived in more than one hundred flag-bearing vehicles for the opening of the party office at Ayodhyapattinam. They removed a protective fence from government-owned poramboke land and used it for parking. Newspapers carried a photograph of a government employee, a nutritious-meal organiser, canvassing votes with ministers.
In violation of the rules, images of the Chief Minister remained across Salem district at Amma canteens, Amma drinking-water outlets bearing the two-leaves symbol, and group houses.
A Block Development Officer visited a minister's house for consultations in violation of election rules. Another officer and two assistants travelled with him in a Tamil Nadu government vehicle.

In Minnampalli, ministers brought agricultural labourers by minibus, served them upma and pongal, made them stand in line and gave each person 2,000 rupees. They chased away the flying squad that went to photograph it.
At the height of all this, newspapers openly reported that every voter was given 2,000 rupees. Television channels showed the scenes.

The Chief Minister campaigned in the constituency and showered voters with promises. The party complained that election rules prohibited those in power from making such promises. After an inquiry, the Election Commission merely warned that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's explanation was unacceptable. Although it was a grave wrong both for the Chief Minister to make those promises and then give the Election Commission a written explanation contrary to the truth, it took no action against her.

The party contested despite all this. But money power, lawlessness and authority created a situation in which our candidate lost the opportunity to win. The very AIADMK that lost its deposit and fell to third place at Pennagaram is now Tamil Nadu's ruling party!

As I said yesterday, this was a victory the AIADMK obtained by distributing vast amounts of money to voters. Let us turn defeats like this into steps towards future victory and continue our work without losing heart!
With affection,
M.K.
